title: Shadows of Brimstone - Part 3 - A Look at the Core Sets (Part Two)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6BegJIenaBE

Daniel (@dungeondive) turns his attention to the map tiles across all three Shadows of Brimstone core sets, examining how the game's physical environments have evolved.

The original Western sets feature double-sided tiles for the mines and their respective otherworlds (Swamps of Giargano and Targa Plateau). Functional but somewhat bland. The Forbidden Fortress tiles mark a clear step up—deeper colours, more interesting layouts, and the gloriously grotesque Belly of the Beast otherworld with its organic, biological aesthetic. A standout addition is the passage encounter deck, which makes even the connecting corridors dangerous.

The real star of this episode is the advanced encounter system. Certain map tiles trigger specific narrative encounters with stat checks and consequences—an underground waterfall that masks approaching enemies, a lake of caustic acid that burns your legs. Daniel reveals his clever organisational hack: double-sleeving the encounter card behind its corresponding map tile so both are drawn together. He also advocates his broader philosophy of playing with curated subsets—one core set plus one otherworld expansion—rather than mixing everything at once. This keeps the game manageable while still offering four distinct environments to explore.

----

How important is map tile art to your dungeon crawling experience—functional abstraction or atmospheric immersion?
